OverviewCourse Description
AI prompt engineering is a 
powerful skill that enables 
individuals to leverage AI 
technologies without extensive 
coding abilities. 
By carefully crafting prompts, 
users can guide large-language 
models (like GPT-3, GPT-4, Google 
Bard, and Copilot) to produce 
valuable outputs, aiding in the 
development of innovative ideas 
and applications. 
This practice democratizes 
access to technology, reducing 
the need for deep technical 
expertise and lowering entry 
barriers for entrepreneurs. 
As AI continues to evolve, prompt 
engineering is likely to become a 
crucial skill, offering significant 
opportunities for those who 
choose to engage with it.Course Outcomes
This course aims to equip students with a robust understanding of the fundamental 
concepts and methodologies applicable to any Large Language Model (LLM). Participants 
wil

l gain a deep knowledge of AI-assisted coding and data analysis, along with a 
comprehensive framework for developing, testing, and managing prompts. 
This course is ideal for students looking to leverage advanced AI technology to enhance 
their work quality across various industries. By effectively utilizing LLMs, potential 
developers can accomplish tasks with exceptional quality and efficiency, showcasing 
enhanced capabilities. 
The course offers an in-depth exploration of the AI field, equipping learners with skills that 
are applicable both professionally and personally. The principles taught will enable 
effective interaction with AI systems, leading to transformative results that were once 
unimaginable.Course Objectives
Understand the fundamentals of prompt engineering and the pivotal role of prompt engineers in systems powered by 
Generative AI and Natural Language Processing (NLP).
Develop in-depth knowledge of Large Language Models (LLMs) and their operational mechanisms. 
Master the skill of crafting, optimizing, and customizing prompts tailored to various AI models, and explore a range of 
prompting concepts and techniques.
Acquire the ability to fine-tune LLMs to pro

duce structured outputs and enhance knowledge retrieval, and create AI-driven 
applications with specific missions.
Learn to identify and address common issues in LLMs, such as hallucinations, and devise innovative solutions.
Gain proficiency in the Testing and Evaluating System Prompts and their effectivity in improving problem-solving capabilities in 
AI-driven apps.
Learn prompt hacking solutions and efficiently increase app security through prompting techniques and fail-safes.  
Understand the ethical considerations and societal implications for responsible AI development and deployment.Tools
This course is designed to be hands-on, offering practical insights 
and exercises. As we progress, students will need to familiarize 
with several tools:
• OpenAI Python library: You will dive into the core of OpenAI’s 
LLMs and learn how to interact and fine-tune models to achieve 
precise outputs tailored to specific needs.
• Promptfoo: You will master the art of crafting effective prompts. 
Throughout the guide, we’ll use Promptfoo to test and score 
prompts, ensuring they’re optimized for desired outcomes.
• LangChain: You’ll explore the LangChain framework, which 
elevates LLM-powere

d applications. You’ll dive into 
understanding how a prompt engineer can leverage the power 
of this tool to test and build effective prompts.
• Betterprompt: Before deploying, it’s essential to test. With 
Betterprompt, you’ll ensure the LLM prompts are ready for real-
world scenarios, refining them as needed.
• Azure Prompt Flow: You will experience the visual interface of 
Azure’s tool, streamlining LLM-based AI development. You’ll 
design executable flows, integrating LLMs, prompts, and Python 
tools, ensuring a holistic understanding of the art of prompting.

What is it, and how does it work?AI vs ML vs DL
Artificial Intelligence (AI) 
Definition: AI is the broadest concept and refers to machines designed to mimic human intelligence. This includes problem-
solving, learning, and decision-making. 
Examples: Virtual assistants like Siri and Alexa, self-driving cars, and recommendation systems.
Machine Learning (ML) 
Definition: ML is a subset of AI that focuses on the development of algorithms that allow computers to learn from and make 
predictions based on data. It improves automatically through experience. 
Examples: Spam email filtering, fraud detection, and personalized recommendations on stream

ing services.
Deep Learning (DL) 
Definition: DL is a subset of ML that uses neural networks with many layers (hence “deep”) to analyze various factors of 
data. It is particularly effective for large datasets and complex tasks. 
Examples: Image and speech recognition, natural language processing, and autonomous driving.
Relationship
Hierarchy: AI > ML > DL. Data Science (DS) intersects with all three, using their techniques to analyze and interpret data.
Complexity: DL involves more complex structures and computations compared to ML, which in turn is more specialized 
than general AI. DS uses methods from AI, ML, and DL to solve real-world problems.

What is it and How does it work?What is NLP?
• Natural Language Processing 
(NLP): This is a subfield of 
artificial intelligence (AI) and 
computer science that focuses on 
the interaction between 
computers and human 
language. It involves enabling 
computers to understand, 
interpret, and generate human 
language through machine 
learning and computational 
linguistics2 3
How NLP Works
Text Preprocessing: 
Before any analysis, text data needs to be cleaned and 
prepared. This involves several steps:
•Tokenization: Splitting text into smaller units like words or 
sentences.
•Lowercasing: Converting all text to lowercase to ensure 
uniformity.
•Removing Stop Words: Eliminating common words (like 
“and”, “the”, “is”) that don’t carry significant meaning.
•Stemming and Lemmatization: Reducing words to their base 
or root form (e.g., “running” to “run”).
Feature Extraction: 
Once the text is preprocessed, the next step is to convert it 
into a format that machine learning models can understand. 
This involves:
•Bag of Words (BoW): Representing text as a set of words, 
disregar

ding grammar and word order but keeping 
multiplicity.
•TF-IDF (Term Frequency-Inverse Document Frequency): 
Weighing the importance of a word in a document relative to 
its frequency across all documents.
•Word Embeddings: Using techniques like Word2Vec or 
GloVe to represent words in a continuous vector space where 
similar words have similar vectors.
•Named Entity Recognition (NER): 
NER identifies and classifies entities in text into categories 
such as names of people, organizations, locations, dates, 
and more. For example, in the sentence “Barack Obama was 
born in Hawaii,” NER would identify “Barack Obama” as a 
person and “Hawaii” as a location.
•Part-of-Speech Tagging: 
This task involves labeling each word in a sentence with its 
corresponding part of speech, such as noun, verb, adjective, 
etc. For example, in the sentence “The quick brown fox jumps 
over the lazy dog,” each word would be tagged accordingly.
•Coreference Resolution: 
Coreference resolution identifies when different expressions 
in a text refer to the same entity. For example, in the 
sentences “Alice went to the park. She enjoyed it,” the 
system would recognize that “She” refers to “Alice.”
Model Build

ing: 
With the features extracted, machine learning models can be

model is then evaluated using metrics like accuracy, precision, recall, and F1 score.

refine the output. For example, in text generation, ensuring grammatical 
correctness and coherence.

insights for strategic decision-making. This helps businesses stay competitive and responsive to market changes.Challenges in NLP
Ambiguity: 
Human language is inherently ambiguous. 
Words and sentences can have multiple 
meanings depending on the context. For 
example, the word “bank” can refer to a 
financial institution or the side of a river. 
Resolving such ambiguities is a significant 
challenge for NLP systems.
Context Understanding: 
Understanding the context in which words 
are used is crucial for accurate language 
processing. Context can change the 
meaning of words and sentences, and 
capturing this context requires 
sophisticated models. For instance, the 
phrase “I saw her duck” can mean different 
things based on the context.
Sarcasm and Irony: 
Detecting sarcasm and irony is difficult for 
NLP models because they often rely on 
subtle cues and context that are hard to 
capture. For example, the sentence “Oh 
great, another rainy day” might be

interpreted as positive without 
understanding the sarcastic tone.
Language Variability: 
Human language is highly variable, with 
different dialects, slang, and 
colloquialisms. NLP models need to handle 
this variability to understand and generate 
text accurately. This includes dealing with 
regional variations and evolving language 
use.
Data Quality and Quantity: 
High-quality, annotated data is essential for 
training effective NLP models. However, 
obtaining large datasets with accurate 
annotations can be challenging and 
expensive. Additionally, some languages 
and domains may have limited data 
available.
Bias and Fairness: 
NLP models can inherit biases present in 
the training data, leading to unfair or 
discriminatory outcomes. For example, a 
model trained on biased data might 
produce biased translations or sentiment 
analysis results. Addressing these biases is 
crucial for creating fair and ethical NLP 
systems.
Multilingual Processing: 
Developing NLP models that work across 
multiple languages is challenging due to 
differences in grammar, syntax, and 
vocabulary. Multilingual models need to 
understand and generate text in various 
languages while maintaining

accuracy and 
coherence.
Real-Time Processing: 
Many NLP applications, such as virtual 
assistants and chatbots, require real-time 
processing of text. Ensuring that models 
can process and respond to text quickly 
and accurately is a technical challenge, 
especially for complex tasks.
Long-Range Dependencies:
 Understanding long-range dependencies in 
text, where the meaning of a word or phrase 
depends on distant parts of the text, is 
difficult. For example, in a long document, 
a reference to “he” might depend on a 
name mentioned several paragraphs 
earlier.
Domain Adaptation: 
NLP models trained on general data may 
not perform well in specific domains, such 
as legal or medical texts. Adapting models 
to work effectively in specialized domains 
requires additional training and fine-tuning.
Privacy and Security: 
Processing sensitive information, such as 
personal data in healthcare or financial 
texts, raises privacy and security concerns. 
Ensuring that NLP systems handle data 
securely and comply with regulations is 
essential.
Evaluation Metrics: 
Evaluating the performance of NLP models 
is challenging because language 
understanding and generation are complex 
tasks. St

andard metrics like accuracy or F1 
score may not fully capture the quality of 
the model’s output, especially for tasks like 
text generation or translation.Breakthroughs in NLP

What is it and How does it work?What is a Model?
Models are intelligent computer programs that 
can perform a specific task. 
For example, a model can be trained to 
recognize images of cats and dogs, to write 
social media posts or blog posts, to provide 
medical assistance or legal advice, and so on.
These models are the result of a t

raining 
process that uses large datasets to teach the 
model how to perform a specific task. The larger 
the dataset, the more accurate the model will 
be. This is why models trained on large datasets 
are often more accurate than models trained on 
smaller datasets.
Using the dataset used for training, models 
acquire the capability to make predictions on 
new data. For example, a model trained on a 
dataset of images of cats and dogs can predict 
whether a new image contains a cat or a dog.What are Foundation Models?
Definition
Foundation models are large-
scale neural networks trained 
on vast amounts of data. They 
serve as a base or "foundation" 
for a multitude of 
applications. Unlike traditional 
AI models that are designed for 
specific tasks, foundation 
models can be fine-tuned for a 
wide range of applications, 
making them incredibly 
versatile.
Example: GPT-3, a large 
language model, can generate 
human-like text, write essays, 
answer questions, and even 
create poetry.
Characteristics 
Foundation models are 
characterized by their scale, 
achieved through several key 
factors:
Hardware Improvements: More 
powerful GPUs (Graphics 
Processing Units) enable the 
trai

ning of larger models.
Transformer Architecture: Turn 
raw data (text, audio, satellite 
images) into compressed 
representations capturing the 
data's basic structure, allowing 
for fine-tuning for specificity.
Massive Datasets: Foundation 
models are trained on enormous 
amounts of data, which can be 
tens of gigabytes to petabytes in 
size.
Training Methods
Traditional Training: The 
model learns from patterns in 
the data.
Transfer Learning: Knowledge 
learned from one task is 
applied to another, enhancing 
the model's adaptability.
Example: GPT-3 was trained 
on 45 terabytes of data and 
uses 175 billion parameters to 
generate coherent and 
contextually relevant text.
Risks and 
Challenges
Despite their potential, 
foundation models also pose 
several risks:
Bias: They can perpetuate 
biases present in their training 
data.
Security: They may become 
targets for cyber attackers.
Environmental Impact: The 
extensive training 
requirements contribute to a 
large environmental footprint.Traditional vs Foundation Models

• In a Textbook Chapter: The use of bold text and italics helps highlight key points; 
photos of key figures add a personal component; graphs and charts present factual 
data; while the layout of the page itself facilitates transmission.
• In a Movie Scene: Lighting and color scheme of the scene, angle of the camera, and 
the soundtrack are all essential mod

es to meaning-making in a movie.
• In a Speech: The speaker’s choice of words, rhythm and tone of voice, rate of 
speech, facial expressions and hand gestures all help convey meaning.
• In a Photo: The use of light and shade, color saturation, angle of the shot, degree of 
focus, and images within the photo itself all combine to create meaning.
• In a Text Message: The choice of words, length of message, use of punctuation, 
emojis and accompanying GIFs, when put together, create a meaning that is whole. 
The meaning could be significantly altered if certain elements were changed.
• In a University Course: A single class may include oratory, information presented 
via a PPT that includes text and graphics, and a video that contains imagery, sound, 
and data animation.
• In Guided Imagery: Guided imagery is a form of meditation where a therapist 
verbalizes instructions to a client in a soothing tone of voice. The client visualizes 
what is being described while music plays in the background and the room is filled 
with aromas from essential oils.  
• In Website Design: Websites can contain multiple modes of communication. Text, 
of course, possibly sound, plus visuals in the form o

f static or moving images from 
videos and data visualizations. 
Multi-Modal Models
Multimodal models are AI 
models that can process and 
integrate multiple types of data, 
known as modalities, such as 
text, images, audio, and video. 
This allows them to understand 
and generate more 
comprehensive and contextually 
rich outputs.Components of Multi-Modal Models
Encoders: These transform raw data 
from each modality into machine-
readable feature vectors or embeddings.
Image Encoders: Often use Convolutional Neural 
Networks (CNNs) to convert image pixels into 
feature vectors.
Text Encoders: Use transformer models to convert 
text into embeddings.
Audio Encoders: Convert audio files into feature 
vectors that capture critical audio patterns.
Fusion Mechanism: This combines the 
features extracted from each modality to 
create a unified representation.
Example: Combining text and image features to 
understand the context of a captioned image.
Decoders: These generate the final 
output based on the fused data.
Example: Generating a textual description of an 
image or creating an image from a textual 
description.Multi-modal Data Processing
Image Data: Convolutional Neural Networks

(CNNs) are commonly used for processing image data. CNNs can 
detect patterns, edges, and textures in images, making them ideal for tasks like image classification, object 
detection, and image generation.
Audio Data: Recurrent Neural Networks (RNNs) and their variants, such as Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) 
networks, are often used for processing audio data. These networks can handle sequential data, making them 
suitable for tasks like speech recognition and audio generation.
Video Data: Video data is processed using a combination of CNNs and RNNs. CNNs extract spatial features from 
individual frames, while RNNs or 3D CNNs capture temporal dynamics across frames. This approach is used in 
video classification, action recognition, and video generation.
Sensor Data: Data from sensors (e.g., LiDAR, radar) is processed using specialized architectures that can handle 
the specific characteristics of the data. For example, point cloud data from LiDAR can be processed using 
PointNet or other 3D CNN architectures.Multi-modal Data Integration 
Early Fusion: Combining 
raw data from different 
modalities before feeding 
it into the model.
Late Fusion: Combining 
the outputs of separate

models trained on 
different modalities.
Hybrid Fusion: 
Combining features at 
multiple stages of the 
model to leverage both 
early and late fusion 
benefits.Handling Missing Data
Shared-Specific Feature 
Modeling: This approach 
involves learning shared 
features that are common 
across all modalities and 
specific features unique to 
each modality. By doing so, 
the model can still make 
predictions even when some 
modalities are missing .
Imputation Techniques: 
Missing data can be imputed 
using statistical methods or 
machine learning models. For 
example, if image data is 
missing, the model might use 
information from text or audio 
to generate a plausible 
substitute.
Auxiliary Tasks: Models can 
be trained on auxiliary tasks 
that help them learn robust 
representations. These tasks 
can include distribution 
alignment and domain 
classification, which help the 
model generalize better even 
when some modalities are 
missing.
Residual Feature Fusion: 
This technique involves 
combining features from 
available modalities in a way 
that compensates for the 
missing data. Residual 
connections can help 
integrate information from 
different sources more 
effectively.
Train

ing with Missing Data: 
Models can be explicitly 
trained to handle missing 
data by simulating missing 
modalities during training. 
This helps the model learn to 
make predictions even when 
some inputs are unavailable.

the field of artificial intelligence.

language based on defined rules and structures, 
enabling machines to comprehend grammar, syntax, and 
context.

e amounts of

weaknesses, with NLP focusing on accuracy and LLMs 
being more adaptable but potentially carrying over biases 
from their training data.

Training on Multilingual Datasets: LLMs are trained on vast datasets that include text from multiple languages. 
This exposure allows them to learn the syntax, grammar, and vocabulary of different languages. For example, 
mo

dels like GPT-4 and BERT have been trained on multilingual corpora, enabling them to understand and 
generate text in various languages.
Language Identification: LLMs can identify the language of the input text. This is crucial for processing 
multilingual content accurately. Once the language is identified, the model can apply the appropriate linguistic 
rules and context.
Translation Capabilities: LLMs can translate text between languages. They use their understanding of both 
source and target languages to provide accurate translations. This is particularly useful for applications like real-
time translation services and multilingual chatbots.
Contextual Understanding: LLMs maintain context across different languages. This means they can understand 
the context of a conversation or document even if it switches between languages. This ability is essential for 
tasks like multilingual customer support and content generation.
Cross-Lingual Transfer Learning: LLMs leverage knowledge from one language to improve performance in 
another. This is known as cross-lingual transfer learning. For instance, learning about medical terminology in 
English can help the model understand and gene

rate medical content in Spanish.
Handling Code-Switching: Code-switching refers to the practice of alternating between two or more languages 
within a conversation or sentence. LLMs can handle code-switching by recognizing and appropriately processing 
the different languages used.
Fine-Tuning for Specific Languages: LLMs can be fine-tuned on specific languages or dialects to improve their 
performance. This involves additional training on datasets that focus on the target language, enhancing the 
model’s accuracy and fluency in that language.Challenges 
of LLMs
Ethical Concerns: 
There are various ethical issues associated with 
LLMs, including, 1) Misinformation by 
generating plausible but incorrect or misleading 
information, 2) Privacy since they might 
inadvertently reveal sensitive information 
present in their training data, and 3) 
Manipulation by misuse in generating fake 
news, deepfakes, or other deceptive content.
Resource Intensity: 
Training and deploying LLMs require substantial 
computational resources and energy, which can 
be costly and environmentally impactful. This 
also limits access to these models to 
organizations with significant resources.
Interpretabili

ty: 
LLMs operate as “black boxes,” meaning their 
decision-making processes are not easily 
interpretable. This lack of transparency can be 
problematic in critical applications where 
understanding the rationale behind decisions is 
essential.
Generalization: 
While LLMs are trained on diverse datasets, they 
may still struggle with generalizing to new, 
unseen contexts or niche domains. Fine-tuning 
is often required to adapt them to specific tasks 
or industries.
Data Quality: 
The quality of the training data significantly 
impacts the performance of LLMs. LLMs can 
inadvertently learn and propagate biases present 
in their training data. Poor-quality or 
unrepresentative data can lead to suboptimal 
performance and biased outputs, which is a 
significant concern in applications like hiring, 
lending, and law enforcement. 
Scalability: 
As LLMs grow in size, managing and deploying 
them becomes more complex. This includes 
challenges related to model updates, version 
control, and ensuring consistent performance 
across different environments.
Security: 
LLMs can be vulnerable to adversarial attacks, 
where malicious inputs are designed to trick the 
model into producing incor

rect or harmful 
outputs. Ensuring robust security measures is 
crucial to mitigate these risks.
Human-AI Interaction:
Ensuring that LLMs interact with humans in a 
way that is understandable, trustworthy, and 
beneficial is an ongoing challenge. This includes 
designing interfaces and feedback mechanisms 
that facilitate effective human-AI collaboration.
Regulation and Compliance: 
Navigating the regulatory landscape for AI 
technologies is complex. Ensuring that LLMs 
comply with data protection laws, ethical

What is an N-gram: An N-gram is a contiguous sequence of ( n ) items from a given sample of text or speech. The items can be words, characters, or other 
units, depending on the application. For example:
•Unigram: A single word (e.g., “This”)
•Bigram: A sequence of two words (e.g., “This is”)
•Trigram: A sequence of three words (e.g., “This is a”)
•4-gram: A sequence of four words (e.g., “This is a test”)
How they Work: N-gram models predict the probability of a word based on the previous ( n-1 ) words. For example, in a trigram model, the probability of the 
wor

d “test” in the sequence “This is a test” is calculated based on the preceding words “This is a” .
Probability Calculation: The probability of a word sequence can be calculated using the chain rule of probability. For a trigram model, the probability of a 
sequence of words ( w_1, w_2, w_3, w_4 ) is:
•P(w1,w2,w3,w4) = P(w1) x P(w2∣w1) x P(w3∣w1,w2) x P(w4∣w1,w2,w3)
Applications: N-gram models are used in various NLP applications, including:
•Speech Recognition: Predicting the next word in a sequence to improve accuracy.
•Machine Translation: Translating text by predicting the most likely sequence of words.
•Text Prediction: Suggesting the next word or phrase in text input (e.g., predictive text on smartphones).
Challenges: Simple to implement and understand, and effective for many practical applications, but limited context (only considers ( n-1 ) previous words), 
can be computationally expensive for large ( n ), and struggles with long-range dependencies.

A transformer model is a type of deep learning model introduced in the 2017 paper “Attention is All You 
Need” by researchers at Google1.
It uses a mechanism called self-attention to weigh the importan

ce of different words in a sentence, allowing 
it to capture long-range dependencies more effectively than previous models like RNNs and LSTMs2.
Self-Attention Mechanism: This allows the model to focus on different parts of the input sequence when 
producing each part of the output sequence. It helps in understanding the context and relationships between 
words.
Positional Encoding: Since transformers do not process data sequentially, positional encoding is used to 
give the model information about the position of words in the sequence.
Encoder-Decoder Architecture: The original transformer model consists of an encoder that processes the 
input sequence and a decoder that generates the output sequence. However, many modern transformers, 
like BERT, use only the encoder, while models like GPT use only the decoder2.
Applications: Primary applications include text generation, machine translation, text summarization, and 
question answering.
Advantages: Unlike RNNs, transformers can support parallel processing, making them faster and more efficient. 
They can capture relationships between distant words in a text. Transformers can be scaled up to handle very large 
datasets, which is cr

ucial for training large language models like GPT-3 and BERT3.

The tokenizer algorithm breaks

Text Segmentation: The tokenizer breaks down the input text into 
tokens. These tokens can be words, subwords, or even individual 
characters, depending on the tokenization method used.
Handling Out-of-Vocabulary (OOV) Words: Tokenization helps 
manage words that are not present in the training vocabulary by 
breaking them down into subwords or characters. This ensures 
that the model can still process and understand these words.
Standardization: The process converts text into a uniform form, 
simplifying further processing and analysis. This standardization 
is essential for handling the diverse structure and format of 
natural language.Common Tokenization Techniques
Byte Pair Encoding (BPE):
• BPE is a widely used 
subword tokenization 
technique in GPT models. It 
iteratively merges the most 
frequent pairs of bytes 
(characters) to create n

ew 
subword units until a 
predefined vocabulary size 
is reached.
• Example: The word 
“unhappiness” might be 
tokenized into “un” , “happi” , 
and “ness” .
WordPiece:
• WordPiece is another 
subword tokenization 
method that builds a 
vocabulary of subwords 
based on their frequency in 
the training data.
• Example: The word 
“playing” might be tokenized 
into “play” and “ing”.
SentencePiece:
• SentencePiece is a more 
flexible tokenization method 
that can handle languages 
without clear word 
boundaries, such as 
Chinese or Japanese.
• Example: The sentence “我
喜欢学习” (I like studying) 
might be tokenized into “我”, 
“喜欢”, and “学习”.Importance of Tokenization
Efficiency: Tokenized data allows models to process text more 
efficiently by focusing on essential components without being 
bogged down by extraneous details.
Parallel Processing: Smaller tokens can be distributed across 
multiple computational units, enabling faster analysis and 
processing.
Model Performance: Proper tokenization enhances the model’s 
ability to learn and generate text by providing a standardized and 
manageable input format.GPT-4 Token Limits

• Data Collection: The model is trained on a large corpus of text data from diverse sources like books, articles, and websites.
• Tokenization: The text is broken down into smaller units called tokens. These can be words, subwords, or characters.
• Learning Objectives: The primary objective during pre-training is to predict the next token in a sequence. This is known as the 
language modeling task.
Pre-training Phase:
• Forward Pass: The input tokens are passed through the model, which generates a probability distribution over the vocabulary for the 
next token.
• Loss Calculation: The model calculates the loss, which measures the difference between the predicted probability distribution and 
the actual distribution (cross-entropy loss).
• Backward Pass: The model updates its parameters to minimize the loss using optimization algorithms like gradient descent.
Training Phase:
• Task-Specific Data: After pre-training, the model is fine-tuned on a smaller, task-specific dataset. This helps the model adapt to 
specific tasks like text generation, translation, or summarization.
• Supervised Learning: During fine-tuning, the mode

l is trained in a supervised manner, where it learns to map inputs to outputs based 
on labeled examples.
• Evaluation and Adjustment: The model’s performance is evaluated on a validation set, and adjustments are made to improve 
accuracy and generalization.

introduce variability.Sampling in GPT
Greedy Sampling: Always selects the token with the highest probability. This can lead to 
repetitive and less creative text.
Top-k Sampling: Limits the sampling pool to the top k tokens with the highest probabilities. 
This introduces more diversity while maintaining quality.
Top-p (Nucleus) Sampling: Selects tokens from the smallest possible set whose cumulative 
pr

obability exceeds a threshold p. This method balances diversity and coherence.
Temperature: Adjusts the probability distribution. A higher temperature increases 
randomness, while a lower temperature makes the model more confident in its predictions.API Parameters
Prompt: The initial text input that you provide to the model. This sets the context for the model’s response. Example:

Max Tokens: The maximum number of tokens (words or subwords) that the model will generate in the response. Example: 
100 (limits the response to 100 tokens)
Temperature: Controls the randomness of the output. A higher temperature (e.g., 1.0) makes the output more random, 
while a lower temperature (e.g., 0.2) makes it more deterministic. Example: 0.7 (balances randomness and determinism)
Top-p (Nucleus Sampling): Controls the cumulative probability threshold for token selection. The model considers the 
smallest set of tokens whose cumulative probability is at least p. Example: 0.9 (selects from the top 90% probability mass)

okens based on their existing frequency in the text so far. This reduces the likelihood of

1. Start with an Action 
Verb: 
Guideline: Begin your prompt 
with a clear action verb to 
specify what you want the AI 
to do. This helps set a clear 
expectation for the task.
Example: Instead of saying, "I 
need a summary of this 
article," say, "Summarize this 
article."
2. Provide a Clear 
Context:
Guideline: Give the AI enough 
context to understand the 
task. This includes 
background information, the 
purpose of the task, and any 
relevant details.
Example: Instead of saying, 
"Explain renewable energy," 
say, "Explain the benefits of 
renewable energy for a general 
audience."
3. Use Role-Playing
Guideline: Assign a specific 
role to the AI to guide its 
responses. This helps the AI 
tailor its response to the 
expected expertise and tone.
Example: Instead of saying, 
"Describe the legal process," 
say, "You are a patent lawyer. 
Describe the legal process for 
patenting an invention."
4. Use References
Guideline: Include references 
or examples to guide the AI. 
This helps the AI understand 
the style and format

you are 
looking for.
Example: Instead of saying, 
"Write a product review," say, 
"Write a product review similar 
to the ones on Amazon."
5. Use Double Quotes
Guideline: Use double quotes 
to indicate specific phrases or 
terms that should be included 
in the response.
Example: Instead of saying, 
"Explain machine learning," 
say, "Explain the concept of 
'machine learning' in simple 
terms."General Guidelines (2)

Needed:

Practices

Prompting techniques are 
strategies used to guide AI models 
in generating desired responses.
These techniques help improve the

PromptingZero-Shot Prompting
TECHNIQUE WHERE A MODEL IS GIVEN A 
TASK WITHOUT ANY PRIOR EXAMPLES OR 
SPECIFIC TRAINING FOR THAT TASK. 
ESSENTIALLY , THE MODEL IS EXPECTED TO 
PERFORM THE TASK “FROM SCRATCH” BASED 
ON ITS GENERAL UNDERSTANDING.
THE MODEL RELIES ON ITS PRE-EXISTING 
KNOWLEDGE TO GENERATE A RESPONSE.Applications of Zero-Shot Prompting
Text Classification: A model can categorize text into different topics or sentiments without being explicitly trained on 
labeled examples. For instance, it can identify whether a review is positive or negative based on its general understanding of 
language.
Question Answering: When asked a factual question, a model can provide an answer based on its pre-existing knowledge, 
even if it hasn’t seen similar questions before. For example, if you ask, “What is the capital of France?” the model can 
respond with “Paris”

without needing prior examples.
Translation: As mentioned earlier, a model can translate text from one language to another without specific training on 
translation pairs. It uses its understanding of both languages to generate the translation.
Creative Writing: A model can generate stories, poems, or other creative content based on a prompt. For example, if you 
ask it to write a short story about a dragon, it can create a narrative without needing examples of dragon stories.
Summarization: A model can summarize long texts into shorter versions, capturing the main points without being trained on 
specific summarization tasks.Examples of Zero-Shot Prompting
Context Prompt Response

loved this product! It exceeded all my expectations. ’ Positive
Question Answering Who was the first president of the United States? George Washington

learning how to code. ’ J’apprends à coder.

Versatility: Zero-shot

Efficiency: It reduces the

Scalability: Models can be

w

Cost-Effective: By

specific training, zero-shot

with training and maintaining

Zero-Shot 
Prompting
Accuracy: Zero-shot 
models may not always 
perform as accurately as 
models trained on specific 
tasks, especially for 
complex or highly 
specialized tasks.
Generalization Limits: 
While zero-shot models 
can generalize well, there 
are limits to their ability to 
handle tasks that are 
vastly different from their

Interpretability: 
Understanding why a zero-
shot model makes certain

Resource Intensive:

terms of computational 
power and storage.Few-Shot

Few-shot prompting is a technique where a model i

s 
provided with a small number of examples to guide its 
response to a specific task. 
One-shot prompting is where the model is provided 
with one example as guidance.
This method sits between zero-shot learning (where no 
examples are given) and fully supervised fine-tuning 
(which requires large amounts of labeled data).
By providing examples in your prompt you're showing 
the model exactly what you are looking for in terms of 
output structure, tone, and style.How it works?
Providing Examples: 
The prompt includes a few input-
output pairs that demonstrate the 
desired behavior. These examples help 
the model understand the task better.
Training the Model: 
The examples in the prompt help train 
the model on what the output should 
look and sound like. This improves the 
LLM’s ability to learn and generalize 
information from a small amount of 
data. It is particularly helpful when 
isn’t enough data to fine-tune a model.
Generalization: 
The model uses these examples to 
generalize and generate appropriate 
responses for new, similar inputs.Example of N-Shot

Benefits of CoT Prompting
Breaks down complex problems: Chain of Thought prompting allows large language models (LLMs) to break down 
complex problems into a series of intermediate steps. This step-by-step approach helps the model focus more on each 
part of the problem, potentially leading to more accurate reasoning.
Transparency: By revealing the reasoning steps the model takes, users can better understand the model’s logic and 
debug any errors in the reasoning paths

.
Versatility: Chain of Thought prompting has been successfully tested across a diverse range of tasks. Its versatility 
makes it suitable for various tasks that require reasoning.
Easy implementation: There are many ways to implement Chain of Thought prompting, and many of them are quite 
simple.Limitations of CoT Prompting

PromptingFaithful CoT
Faithful Chain-of-Thought (CoT) prompting is a technique designed to 
ensure that the reasoning process used by an AI model to arrive at an 
answer is transparent, accurate, and directly traceable. 
It is designed to ensure that an LLM faithfully follows and uses the 
reasoning chains it generates to arrive at its final answer.How it works
Faithful Chain of Thought ensures that the 
reasoning chain aligns with the final answer 
through a two-step process:
• Translate the Natural Language Query: 
Instead

of relying solely on natural 
language reasoning, Faithful Chain of 
Thought converts the question or task into 
a symbolic format, such as generating a 
Python program.
• Use a Deterministic Solver: Rather than 
having a large language model (LLM) 
follow the reasoning chain generated in 
step 1, a deterministic solver is employed. 
This guarantees that the reasoning chain 
directly leads to the result, ensuring its 
faithfulness1.

chain. 
Problem Solving: The reasoning

Improved Performance: This 
method often outperforms other

choice of exemplars1.

Faithful CoT focuses on ensuring

reflects the model’s internal 
computations, as opposed to just

For some complex

their solution based on the examples provided.
To mitigate this, analogical prompting adds an additional step to the process, that involves 
prompting the model to take a step back and generate high-level information before solving the 
task. This enables the model to think more broadly and abstractly before generating the examples

Example: In a code generation task, the model might initially summarize overarching 
programming concepts before diving into the problem at hand. This primes the model for a more

Auto Chain of Thought (Auto-CoT) prompting is an advanced technique designed to enhance 
the reasoning capabilities of AI models by automating the generation of reasoning chains. 
Auto-CoT was developed to address the challenge of manually creating demonstrations for 
the CoT prompt. It automates the generation of reasoning demonstrations, removing the need 
for manual effort. 
The core concept of Auto-CoT prompting is the importance of diversity in both the questions 
and reasoning demonstrations. 
Auto-CoT prompting begins by selecting diverse examples from a dataset and then generates 
reasoning chains for these demonstrations.Auto-CoT Key

Questions from a given dataset of potential

Does 
Quantity 
Matter?
Quality vs. Quantity: 
While the quality of the generated knowledge is crucial, the quantity also plays a significant 
role. More knowledge statements can provide a richer context, leading to more accurate and 
relevant responses.
Improved Contextual Understanding: 
Including a larger quantity of relevant knowledge helps the LLM build a more comprehensive 
understanding of the task. This is particularly beneficial for complex tasks that require 
detailed background informa

tion.
Balancing Relevance: 
It’s important to balance the quantity of knowledge with its relevance. Too much irrelevant 
information can overwhelm the LLM and lead to less accurate outputs. Therefore, the 
knowledge generated should be carefully curated to ensure it directly supports the task.
Integration Strategy: 
The way knowledge is integrated into the prompt also matters. Effective integration strategies 
can help manage larger quantities of knowledge without overwhelming the model, ensuring 
that the additional information enhances rather than detracts from performance.
Performance Gains: 
Studies have shown that performance improves with more knowledge statements, as long as 
they are relevant and well-integrated. This can lead to higher accuracy and a deeper 
understanding of the task.Example

Limitations of 
NLP
NLP models are trained on extensive text datasets, 
allowing them to generate relevant and logical responses 
based on their training examples. 
However, they don’t truly understand math and logic; they 
rely on recognized patterns to form answers. 
Essentially, they can’t explain their reasoning—they just 
“mix” words to create an eloquent response. 
The Chain-of-Thought (CoT) method, which prompts the 
model to explain its reasoning step-by-step, has shown 
promise in enhancing reasoning, but it’s not without 
flaws.Limitations of CoT
Over-reliance on a Single Path: CoT often uses 
a “greedy” approach, sticking to the first, most 
obvious reasoning path it finds. This can lead to 
errors if that path is flawed or if there’s a better 
solution it hasn’t considered. 
Lack of Diversity: Since CoT tends to follow a 
single line of reasoning, it might miss out on 
other valid ways to approach a problem. This 
can be especially limiting for complex problems 
where multiple perspectives are benefi

cial. 
Not Always Optimal: Just because a model can 
explain its reasoning doesn’t mean that 
reasoning is correct. CoT can sometimes lead 
to verbose or roundabout explanations that 
sound logical but are actually based on 
incorrect assumptions.Decomposition

Exploring Demonstration Ensembling
An Information-Theoretic Approach to 
Prompt EngineeringWhat is Ensembling?
• Ensembling involves using multiple prompts to address the same problem 
and then aggregating the responses into a final output. 
• Often, a majority vote, where the most frequent

response is selected, 
determines the final result. 
• This approach aims to reduce the variance in outputs from LLMs and 
improves the reliability and accuracy of the responses. 
• However, it also increases the number of model calls required to reach a 
final answer, which can be more computationally intensive.Ensembling Methods
• Majority Voting: Each model votes for a specific output, and the one with the most votes is 
the final prediction.
• Weighted Voting: Similar to majority voting, but each model has a predefined weight 
based on its performance.
• Bagging: Each model is trained on a slightly different dataset, and the predictions are 
combined, usually through majority voting or averaging.
• Boosting: A sequential method where each new model aims to correct the mistakes made 
by the previous models.
• Stacking: Multiple base models predict the output, and these predictions are used as 
inputs for a second-layer model, which provides the final prediction.Ensembling Techniques

When working with LLMs, a common 
problem is making sure their responses 
are both accurate and reliable. 
One powerful approach to tackling this

Self-criticism involves prompting the 
LLM to review its initial response, 
identify potential flaws or errors, and 
refine the output accordingly.
Self-Criticism prompting techniques 
are designed to improve the model’s 
performance through self-assessment, 
iterative reasoning, and error detection.Steps in Self-Criticism
•Description: The model generates an initial response to the given prompt.
•Example: For a math problem like "Calculate the area of a triangle with a base of 5 units and a height of 10 units," the initial res ponse might be: "The area of the 
triangle is 12×base×height=12×5×10=2521×base×height=21×5×10=25 square units."
Initial Response Generation:
•Description: The model reviews its initial response to identify any logical flaws, incorrect assumptions, or miss

ing information.
•Example: The model checks the calculation and realizes that while the answer is correct, the explanation could be more detailed.
Self-Review:
•Description: The model refines its response based on the identified issues, iterating this process as needed to enhance accuracy and qua lity.
•Example: The refined response might be: "To calculate the area of a triangle, use the formula Area=12×base×heightArea=21×base×height. For a base of 5 units 
and a height of 10 units, the area is 12×5×10=2521×5×10=25 square units."
Refinement:
•Description: The model verifies the refined response to ensure it addresses the prompt accurately and comprehensively.
•Example: The model rechecks the refined response to confirm that the explanation is clear and the calculation is correct.
Verification:
•Description: The model produces the final, refined response after ensuring it is accurate and complete.
•Example: The final output is: "To calculate the area of a triangle, use the formula Area=12×base×heightArea=21×base×height. For a base of 5 units and a height of 
10 units, the area is 12×5×10=2521×5×10=25 square units. This calculation shows that the area of the triangle is 25 square uni

ts."

Transfer learning (TL), by definition, is the enhancement of learning in a new task through the 
transfer of knowledge from a related task that has already been learned.
Transfer learning is a technique in artificial intelligence (AI) where a model developed for one task is 
reused as the starting point for a model on a second, related task.This approach leverages the 
knowledge gained from the initial task to improve the performance and efficiency of the new task1 2.
An expert pianist that decides to learn the violin, will have an understanding of music theory, rhythm, 
and even finger dexterity from playing the piano, and it will help them learn the violin faster.
Similarly, in AI, a model trained on a large dataset for one task can be fine-tuned for another task 
with a smaller dataset, saving time and computational resources1.Types of Transfer Learning
1
2
3
Inductive Transfer Learning: 
In this type, the source and target tasks 
are different, but the domains can be the 
same or different. The goal is to improve 
t

he performance of the target task using 
knowledge from the source task. For 
example, using a model trained on image 
classification to help with object 
detection1.
TransductiveTransfer Learning: 
Here, the source and target tasks are the 
same, but the domains are different. This 
is often used when the target domain has 
limited labeled data. An example would 
be using a sentiment analysis model 
trained on movie reviews to analyze 
sentiment in product reviews1.
Unsupervised Transfer Learning: 
In this scenario, both the source and 
target tasks are unsupervised, meaning 
they involve tasks like clustering or 
dimensionality reduction. The aim is to 
transfer knowledge from one 
unsupervised task to another1.
Self-Taught Transfer Learning: 
This type involves using a large amount of 
unlabeled data from the source domain 
to learn a good feature representation, 
which is then used to improve the 
performance on the target task with 
limited labeled data2.
Feature Extraction: 
In feature extraction, a pre-trained model 
is used to extract features from new 
data. These features are then used as 
input for a new model tailored to the 
target task3.
Fine-Tuning: 
Fine-tuning invo

lves taking a pre-trained 
model and slightly adjusting its 
parameters to better fit the new task. This 
is particularly useful when the new task is 
similar to the original task the model was 
trained on3.Benefits and Limitations of Transfer Learning

Definition:
Fine-tuning involves taking a pre-trained 
model and training it further on a specific 
dataset to adapt it to a particular task. This 
process adjusts the model’s weights based on 
the new data, allowing it to perform better on 
the specif

ic task. Fine-tuning is often used 
when you have a large, general-purpose model 
and you want to specialize it for a narrower 
application.
Key Points:
Requires additional training: Fine-tuning 
involves further training the model on a new 
dataset.
Task-specific: It adapts the model to perform 
better on a specific task.
Adjusts model weights: The model’s internal 
parameters are updated during the process.How Fine-Tuning Works
1 2
Pre-trained Model: Start with a model that has already been trained on a large dataset. This model 
has learned general features and patterns from this data.
Specific Task: Fine-tuning involves continuing the training of this pre-trained model on a smaller, 
task-specific dataset. This helps the model adjust its parameters to better fit the new data.
Efficiency: This process is more efficient than training a model from scratch because it leverages 
the knowledge the model has already acquired1 2.
Example: if you have a language model trained on a vast amount of general text, you can fine-tune it 
on medical texts to make it more proficient in understanding and generating medical information.Use Cases for Fine-Tuning
